questions.question: Which three annotations does @SpringBootApplication combine?
questions.explanation: @SpringBootApplication is a meta-annotation that bundles @SpringBootConfiguration (this class is a config source), @EnableAutoConfiguration (turn on Boot's auto-config), and @ComponentScan (scan this package and below for components).

questions.question: You add a controller in package com.example.other, but your main application class is in com.example.orders. Hitting the new endpoint returns 404. Why?
choices: @RestController is no longer supported in Spring Boot 3.
@ComponentScan only scans the main class's package and its sub-packages; com.example.other is outside the scan path.
Spring Boot disables routing in dev mode unless explicitly enabled.
Tomcat must be replaced with Jetty to allow custom packages.
questions.explanation: @SpringBootApplication's implicit @ComponentScan starts at the main class's package. Components outside that tree aren't discovered. Either move the package or use @ComponentScan(basePackages = {...}) to widen the scan.

questions.question: You want a fast inner loop with sub-second restarts when editing Java files in IntelliJ. Which combination delivers it for Spring Boot 3?
choices: Add spring-boot-devtools as a runtime dependency and enable IntelliJ's 'Build project automatically' plus 'Allow auto-make to start'.
Use the Spring CLI's hot-reload-server command.
Switch from Maven to Gradle; only Gradle supports hot reload.
questions.explanation: Spring Boot DevTools restarts the application context when classpath resources change, using a two-classloader strategy for sub-second restarts. IntelliJ needs to auto-compile on save for DevTools to see the changes - that's what the two checkboxes enable.
